cluster_name: Ebola outbreak in DRC tops 900 cases amid violence and aid cuts
event_summary: An Ebola outbreak in eastern DR Congo caused by the rare Bundibugyo strain has surpassed 900 suspected cases and spread to Uganda; WHO declared a public health emergency of international concern as arson attacks on treatment centers and international aid cuts complicate containment efforts.
tldr: BBC Africa is the only outlet in 39 headlines to embed direct community voice — 'Ebola has tortured us': Fear grips eastern DR Congo — a register of emotional testimony absent from every other title in the corpus, including all other African-continent outlets. At the opposite pole, French, German, Spanish, and wire-service outlets converge on near-verbatim case-count language — '900 cas suspects,' '900 Verdachtsfälle,' 'más de 900 casos,' 'more than 900 suspected cases' — reflecting wholesale wire-copy adoption that compresses the story to a single WHO statistic. A structural-failure narrative (aid cuts, armed rebels, capacity collapse) breaks through in Canadian, Turkish, UK, and South Asian headlines, creating a two-tier story that readers encounter as either a disease-count or a governance-failure story depending on which outlet they follow. Turkey is the only country bucket to carry both registers simultaneously: one Daily Sabah headline leads with gunmen storming the hospital, the other names aid cuts and armed groups as structural drivers. India's Republic World uniquely refracts the outbreak through domestic policy — 'India Issues Travel Advisory For Three African Nations' — erasing DRC and Uganda from the lede entirely in favor of an Indian foreign-policy frame visible nowhere else in the corpus.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet in the 39-article corpus to embed direct community testimony in the headline — 'Ebola has tortured us': Fear grips eastern DR Congo as deadly virus spreads — using a victim's emotional register that no other outlet, including the other pan-African title AfricaNews, matched at the RSS layer.

Only outlet to headline the story through domestic Indian sovereign action — 'India Issues Travel Advisory For Three African Nations' — entirely replacing DRC and Uganda as protagonists with India's foreign-policy response; the outbreak itself appears only in the sub-deck.

Only outlet to publish both a security-register and a structural-failure headline on the same story within a single day: 'Gunmen storm Ebola hospital in DRC, trigger panic, evacuations' and 'Congo battles Ebola outbreak complicated by aid cuts, armed groups, anger' — a dual coverage emphasis absent from every other outlet in the corpus.

Published 'Bundibugyo ebolavirus | A deadly pathogen' — the only headline in the corpus to omit geography, incident, and statistics entirely in favor of a pure virology-explainer hook, framing the story as a scientific object rather than a geopolitical or humanitarian crisis.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, this story fractures into two largely non-overlapping worlds: a wire-convergence world where French, German, Spanish, Korean, and South African outlets recite near-identical WHO case counts, and an analytical world where North American, British, Turkish, and South Asian outlets foreground the structural causes — aid cuts, armed rebels, treatment-center arson — that transform a containable outbreak into a cascading crisis. The emotional and community-voice register appears in exactly one headline out of 39, confirming that the lived experience of affected Congolese communities is almost entirely suppressed at the RSS/homepage layer even in outlets that carry detailed body-text testimony.
